title: Pleasing God: Enoch’s Testimony
speaker: Pastor Tito Lyro
source_files: 03-30-2025 -AM- Pleasing God - Enoch's Testimony - Hebrews 11_1-6 - Pastor Tito Lyro.txt

outline.title: Enoch’s Place in Scripture
subpoints: The only historical record of Enoch’s life is a brief genealogical note in Genesis 5:18-24
Enoch appears in three other biblical references: the genealogy of Jesus (Luke 3:37), Hebrews 11:5, and Jude 14-15
Jewish fascination with Enoch led to the apocryphal 1 Enoch (71 chapters), which the author of Hebrews consciously bypasses
Jude quotes from 1 Enoch to cite preserved oral tradition of Enoch’s prophecy
The author of Hebrews includes Enoch as the fourth example of faith in chapter 11 (after the elders, the congregation, and Abel)

outline.title: What Made Enoch Special: He Pleased God
subpoints: Enoch was taken to heaven without dying—one of only two people in Scripture to whom this happened (the other being Elijah)
Some link Enoch with the two witnesses in Revelation 11; but those who are alive at Christ’s return are transformed without dying, making this unnecessary
His testimony: he pleased God by trusting God and God’s promises, not by his own righteousness
Hebrews 11:5-6 explains: Enoch’s faith, not his deeds, is what pleased God
Enoch walked with God (echoing Adam’s fellowship in the garden) because he believed in the promised Seed of the woman

outline.title: Hebrews 11:6 as a Universal and Personal Description of Faith
subpoints: Without faith it is impossible to please God (v. 6)—not merely difficult, but impossible
Romans 8:8: the natural man cannot please God
True faith believes that God exists and that He rewards those who seek Him
F.F. Bruce: this is not belief in the existence of ‘a’ god but in the God who spoke to the fathers and has now spoken in His Son
Leon Morris: we must believe not only that God exists, but that God cares
Romans 3:10: there is none righteous, not one—we are not righteous by our own deeds but by Christ’s righteousness imputed to us by faith

outline.title: Three Responses Enoch’s Testimony Calls Us To
subpoints: First: Repent of unbelief. If you have never trusted Christ, trust him now. If you are a believer, repent of particular unbelief and trust every word that proceeds from God’s mouth
Second: Repent of self-righteousness. Do not trust pedigree, baptism, church membership, charitable giving, or moral behavior. God is pleased only by Christ’s righteousness imputed through faith

Third: Walk with God. As Micah 6:8 says: do justly, love mercy, and walk humbly with your God. We walk with God through obedience, listening to his Word, prayer, and fellowship with his people at the Lord’s Table

summary:
Pastor Tito Lyro preaches this standalone Sunday morning sermon on Hebrews 11:1-6, focusing particularly on verses 5-6 and the figure of Enoch as an example of saving faith. The sermon is framed around a single great question: What does it mean to please God?

Enoch is introduced as one of the most mysterious figures in the Bible—known only from a brief genealogical entry in Genesis 5 and a few New Testament references. Jewish traditions elaborated extensively on Enoch (producing the apocryphal 1 Enoch), but the author of Hebrews deliberately bypasses all of that to focus on the single thing the Bible says about him: he pleased God because he believed in God. Pastor Lyro traces Enoch’s appearances in Luke’s genealogy of Jesus, the Hebrews 11 hall of faith, and Jude’s citation of his prophecy, before homing in on the text itself.

The heart of the sermon is the exposition of Hebrews 11:6: without faith it is impossible to please God. The Holy Spirit does not say that without faith it is difficult to please God, but impossible. This is confirmed by Romans 8:8 (the natural man cannot please God) and Romans 3:10 (there is none righteous, not one). Pastor Lyro draws on F.F. Bruce and Leon Morris to define the faith required—not belief in a generic deity but trust in the covenant God who has spoken definitively in his Son, and who is a rewarder of those who diligently seek him. We are righteous before God not because of our deeds but because Christ lived a perfect life, died in our place, and rose from the dead, and we believe that.

The sermon concludes with three calls: repent of unbelief, repent of self-righteousness, and walk with God. The closing hymn text (“Nothing in my hand I bring, simply to thy cross I cling”) captures the sermon’s entire thrust: we come to God as filthy sinners, trusting solely in the work of Christ, and that is what pleases him.
